revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 若一神社 |所在地= [[京都府]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町98 |画像 = |緯度度 = |経度度 = |地図国コード = |祭神 = [[若一王子]] |創建 = [[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）11月10日 |社格 = |札所等 = |本殿 = |例祭 = 例祭 11月10日 |神事 = |地図 = }} '''若一神社'''（にゃくいちじんじゃ）は、[[京都市]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町にある[[神社]]。[[平清盛]]の別邸・西八条殿の跡地にある。
== 歴史 == [[光仁天皇]]の時代に[[唐]]から来た威光上人が[[摂津国]][[天王寺]]に住していた。威光は[[紀伊国]]の[[熊野]]に詣でて人々を救おうとし、[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]の一つである[[若一王子]]の御分霊である御神体を笈に背負って旅立つと、やがて当地に到来した。[[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）のことであった。威光は当地の森にあった古堂に、神意に従って御神体を安置した、これがそもそもの当社の始まりであるという。
しかし、その後異変により、御神体は土の中に入ったという。
[[平安時代]]末期、[[平清盛]]が六波羅邸に在住していた頃に、清盛は風光明媚な当地に別邸・西八条殿を造営した。そして、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）8月に清盛が熊野に詣でた際に熊野権現から「土中に隠れたる御神体、世に出し奉斎せよ」とのお告げがあった。清盛は帰京すると西八条殿の邸内を探索し、東方の築山より光が放たれているのを見つけた。清盛は歓喜して自ら三尺ほど土を掘ってみると土中より若一王子の御神体が現われたので同年11月10日に社を建立して鎮守社として祀った。
すると、翌仁安2年（[[1167年]]）2月10日に[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられた。これにより、当社は開運出世の神様と尊崇されるようになったという。
境内には平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]]があるが、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）に[[京都市]]が[[京都市電]]を延伸させて当社の境内の西に通そうとした際、この楠を切れば清盛の祟りがあるとされたため、楠と当社を避けるようにして市電が通された。市電が廃止された現在でも[[西大路通]]はそこの部分だけ蛇行している。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[若一王子]]（にゃくいちおうじ）
== 境内 == * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 社務所 * [[平清盛]]ゆかりの御神水 * 平清盛像 * 平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]] - 樹齢800年。平清盛が[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられたのを感謝して手植えしたもの。歩道を挟んで当社の向いにある。この楠を避けるようにして[[西大路通]]が通っている。
=== 境内社 === * 寿命社 - 祭神：高砂尉と姥。[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に[[播磨国]]の[[高砂神社]]より勧請。 * 弁財天社 - 祭神：[[イチキシマヒメ|市杵島姫命]]。[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]5年（[[1715年]]）に[[竹生島]]の[[都久夫須麻神社]]より勧請。 * 松尾社 - 祭神：[[大山咋神|松尾大神]] * 稲荷社 - 祭神：[[稲荷神|稲荷大神]]。[[伏見稲荷大社]]より勧請。 * 祖霊社 - 祭神：当神社奉仕の神職及び総代、世話方並に特別崇敬篤き方々の御霊。[[1983年]]（[[昭和]]58年）9月11日建立。 * 楠木社 - 祭神：平清盛御手植の楠
== 祭事 == * 例祭（れいさい） - 11月10日 * 月旦祭（おついたちまいり） - 毎月1日 * 月次祭（つきなみさい） - 毎月10日 * 旬祭（しゅんさい） - 毎月21日
== 参考文献 == * 若一神社由緒
== 関連項目 == * [[若一王子]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ 若一神社]

== 歴史 == [[光仁天皇]]の時代に[[唐]]から来た威光上人が[[摂津国]][[天王寺]]に住していた。威光は[[紀伊国]]の[[熊野]]に詣でて人々を救おうとし、[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]の一つである[[若一王子]]の御分霊である御神体を笈に背負って旅立つと、やがて当地に到来した。[[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）のことであった。威光は当地の森にあった古堂に、神意に従って御神体を安置した、これがそもそもの当社の始まりであるという。
しかし、その後異変により、御神体は土の中に入ったという。
[[平安時代]]末期、[[平清盛]]が六波羅邸に在住していた頃に、清盛は風光明媚な当地に別邸・西八条殿を造営した。そして、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）8月に清盛が熊野に詣でた際に熊野権現から「土中に隠れたる御神体、世に出し奉斎せよ」とのお告げがあった。清盛は帰京すると西八条殿の邸内を探索し、東方の築山より光が放たれているのを見つけた。清盛は歓喜して自ら三尺ほど土を掘ってみると土中より若一王子の御神体が現われたので同年11月10日に社を建立して鎮守社として祀った。
すると、翌仁安2年（[[1167年]]）2月10日に[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられた。これにより、当社は開運出世の神様と尊崇されるようになったという。
境内には平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]]があるが、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）に[[京都市]]が[[京都市電]]を延伸させて当社の境内の西に通そうとした際、この楠を切れば清盛の祟りがあるとされたため、楠と当社を避けるようにして市電が通された。市電が廃止された現在でも[[西大路通]]はそこの部分だけ蛇行している。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[若一王子]]（にゃくいちおうじ）
== 境内 == * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 社務所 * [[平清盛]]ゆかりの御神水 * 平清盛像 * 平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]] - 樹齢800年。平清盛が[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられたのを感謝して手植えしたもの。歩道を挟んで当社の向いにある。この楠を避けるようにして[[西大路通]]が通っている。
=== 境内社 === * 寿命社 - 祭神：高砂尉と姥。[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に[[播磨国]]の[[高砂神社]]より勧請。 * 弁財天社 - 祭神：[[イチキシマヒメ|市杵島姫命]]。[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]5年（[[1715年]]）に[[竹生島]]の[[都久夫須麻神社]]より勧請。 * 松尾社 - 祭神：[[大山咋神|松尾大神]] * 稲荷社 - 祭神：[[稲荷神|稲荷大神]]。[[伏見稲荷大社]]より勧請。 * 祖霊社 - 祭神：当神社奉仕の神職及び総代、世話方並に特別崇敬篤き方々の御霊。[[1983年]]（[[昭和]]58年）9月11日建立。 * 楠木社 - 祭神：平清盛御手植の楠 <gallery> ファイル:NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|thumb|220px|平清盛像 </gallery>
== 祭事 == * 例祭（れいさい） - 11月10日 * 月旦祭（おついたちまいり） - 毎月1日 * 月次祭（つきなみさい） - 毎月10日 * 旬祭（しゅんさい） - 毎月21日
== 参考文献 == * 若一神社由緒
== 関連項目 == * [[若一王子]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ 若一神社]
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:にやくいちしんしや}} [[Category:京都市の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:下京区の建築物]] [[Category:下京区の歴史]] [[Category:平清盛]]

{{神社 |名称= 若一神社 |所在地 = [[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町98 |画像 = [[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 59|緯度秒 = 3.9 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 58.5 |地図国コード = |祭神 = [[若一王子]] |創建 = [[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）11月10日 |社格 = |札所等 = |本殿 = |例祭 = 例祭 11月10日 |神事 = |地図 = Japan Kyoto city }} '''若一神社'''（にゃくいちじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町にある[[神社]]。[[平清盛]]の別邸・西八条殿の跡地にある。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[若一王子]]（にゃくいちおうじ）
== 歴史 == [[光仁天皇]]の時代に[[唐]]から来た威光上人が[[摂津国]][[天王寺]]に住していた。威光は[[紀伊国]]の[[熊野]]に詣でて人々を救おうとし、[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]の一つである[[若一王子]]の御分霊である御神体を笈に背負って旅立つと、やがて当地に到来した。[[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）のことであった。威光は当地の森にあった古堂に、神意に従って御神体を安置した、これがそもそもの当社の始まりであるという<ref name="若一神社">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html 若一神社ホームページ]</ref>。
しかし、その後異変により、御神体は土の中に入ったという<ref name="若一神社"/>。
[[平安時代]]末期、[[平清盛]]が六波羅邸に在住していた頃に、清盛は風光明媚な当地に別邸・西八条殿を造営した。そして、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）8月に清盛が熊野に詣でた際に熊野権現から「土中に隠れたる御神体、世に出し奉斎せよ」とのお告げがあった。清盛は帰京すると西八条殿の邸内を探索し、東方の築山より光が放たれているのを見つけた。清盛は歓喜して自ら三尺ほど土を掘ってみると土中より若一王子の御神体が現われたので同年11月10日に社を建立して鎮守社として祀った<ref name="若一神社"/>。
すると、翌仁安2年（[[1167年]]）2月10日に[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられた。これにより、当社は開運出世の神様と尊崇されるようになったという<ref name="若一神社"/>。
境内には平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]]があるが、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）に[[京都市]]が[[京都市電]]を延伸させて当社の境内の西に通そうとした際、この楠を切れば清盛の祟りがあるとされたため、楠と当社を避けるようにして市電が通された。市電が廃止された現在でも[[西大路通]]はそこの部分だけ蛇行している<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design 若一神社]</ref>。
== 境内 == * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 社務所 * [[平清盛]]ゆかりの御神水 * 平清盛像 * 平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]] - 樹齢800年。平清盛が[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられたのを感謝して手植えしたもの。歩道を挟んで当社の向いにある。この楠を避けるようにして[[西大路通]]が通っている。
=== 境内社 === * 寿命社 - 祭神：高砂尉と姥。[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に[[播磨国]]の[[高砂神社]]より勧請。 * 弁財天社 - 祭神：[[イチキシマヒメ|市杵島姫命]]。[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]5年（[[1715年]]）に[[竹生島]]の[[都久夫須麻神社]]より勧請。 * 松尾社 - 祭神：[[大山咋神|松尾大神]] * 稲荷社 - 祭神：[[稲荷神|稲荷大神]]。[[伏見稲荷大社]]より勧請。 * 祖霊社 - 祭神：当神社奉仕の神職及び総代、世話方並に特別崇敬篤き方々の御霊。[[1983年]]（[[昭和]]58年）9月11日建立。 * 楠木社 - 祭神：平清盛御手植の楠 <gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|平清盛像 </gallery>
== 祭事 == * 例祭（れいさい） - 11月10日 * 月旦祭（おついたちまいり） - 毎月1日 * 月次祭（つきなみさい） - 毎月10日 * 旬祭（しゅんさい） - 毎月21日
== 所在地 == * 京都府京都市下京区七条御所ノ内本町98
== アクセス == * [[JR|西日本旅客鉄道]][[東海道本線]]（[[JR京都線]]） [[西大路駅]]より徒歩約4分

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |Location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 |Image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |Deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |Shrine Rank= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |Annual Festival= Annual Festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== Deity == * Main Deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from [[Takasago Shrine]] in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: Deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: Deity—[[Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: Deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: Deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * Annual Festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== Location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine
== See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

[[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Guardian Shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and Structures in Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[Category:not stubs]]
[[ja:若一神社]]

{{Shrine |Name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |Location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 |Image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |Deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |Shrine Rank= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |Annual Festival= Annual Festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== Deity == * Main Deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from [[Takasago Shrine]] in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: Deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: Deity—[[Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: Deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: Deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * Annual Festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== Location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine
== See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: Deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

[[Category:Shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Guardian Shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and Structures in Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[Category:not stubs]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Nyakuichi Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:若一神社]]

{{Shrine |Name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |Shrine Rank= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |Annual Festival= Annual Festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from [[Takasago Shrine]] in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * Annual Festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

{{Shrine |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from [[Takasago Shrine]] in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. {{Shrine |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine
== See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.

Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>

Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine
== See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 2]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine
== See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)

Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=draft}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=draft}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}
<!--Nyakuichi Shrine-->

Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

revision.comment: 9 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:若一神社 up to rev 100351179
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 若一神社 |所在地 = [[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町98 |画像 = [[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 59|緯度秒 = 3.9 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 58.5 |地図国コード = |祭神 = [[若一王子]] |創建 = [[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）11月10日 |社格 = |札所等 = |本殿 = |例祭 = 例祭 11月10日 |神事 = |地図 = Japan Kyoto city }} '''若一神社'''（にゃくいちじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]七条御所ノ内本町にある[[神社]]。[[平清盛]]の別邸・西八条殿の跡地にある。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[若一王子]]（にゃくいちおうじ）
== 歴史 == [[光仁天皇]]の時代に[[唐]]から来た威光上人が[[摂津国]][[天王寺]]に住していた。威光は[[紀伊国]]の[[熊野]]に詣でて人々を救おうとし、[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]の一つである[[若一王子]]の御分霊である御神体を笈に背負って旅立つと、やがて当地に到来した。[[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）のことであった。威光は当地の森にあった古堂に、神意に従って御神体を安置した、これがそもそもの当社の始まりであるという<ref name="若一神社">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html 若一神社ホームページ]</ref>。
しかし、その後異変により、御神体は土の中に入ったという<ref name="若一神社"/>。
[[平安時代]]末期、[[平清盛]]が六波羅邸に在住していた頃に、清盛は風光明媚な当地に別邸・西八条殿を造営した。そして、[[仁安 (日本)|仁安]]元年（[[1166年]]）8月に清盛が熊野に詣でた際に熊野権現から「土中に隠れたる御神体、世に出し奉斎せよ」とのお告げがあった。清盛は帰京すると西八条殿の邸内を探索し、東方の築山より光が放たれているのを見つけた。清盛は歓喜して自ら三尺ほど土を掘ってみると土中より若一王子の御神体が現われたので同年11月10日に社を建立して鎮守社として祀った<ref name="若一神社"/>。
すると、翌仁安2年（[[1167年]]）2月10日に[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられた。これにより、当社は開運出世の神様と尊崇されるようになったという<ref name="若一神社"/>。
境内には平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]]があるが、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）に[[京都市]]が[[京都市電]]を延伸させて当社の境内の西に通そうとした際、この楠を切れば清盛の祟りがあるとされたため、楠と当社を避けるようにして市電が通された。市電が廃止された現在でも[[西大路通]]はそこの部分だけ蛇行している<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design 若一神社]</ref>。
== 境内 == * 本殿 * 拝殿 * 社務所 * [[平清盛]]ゆかりの御神水 * 平清盛像 * 平清盛御手植の[[クスノキ|楠]] - 樹齢800年。平清盛が[[太政大臣]]に任ぜられたのを感謝して手植えしたもの。歩道を挟んで当社の向いにある。この楠を避けるようにして[[西大路通]]が通っている。
=== 境内社 === * 寿命社 - 祭神：高砂尉と姥。[[天正]]年間（[[1573年]] - [[1592年]]）に[[播磨国]]の[[高砂神社]]より勧請。 * 弁財天社 - 祭神：[[イチキシマヒメ|市杵島姫命]]。[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]5年（[[1715年]]）に[[竹生島]]の[[都久夫須麻神社]]より勧請。 * 松尾社 - 祭神：[[大山咋神|松尾大神]] * 稲荷社 - 祭神：[[稲荷神|稲荷大神]]。[[伏見稲荷大社]]より勧請。 * 祖霊社 - 祭神：当神社奉仕の神職及び総代、世話方並に特別崇敬篤き方々の御霊。[[1983年]]（[[昭和]]58年）9月11日建立。 * 楠木社 - 祭神：平清盛御手植の楠 <gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|平清盛像 </gallery>
== 祭事 == * 例祭（れいさい） - 11月10日 * 月旦祭（おついたちまいり） - 毎月1日 * 月次祭（つきなみさい） - 毎月10日 * 旬祭（しゅんさい） - 毎月21日
== 所在地 == * 京都府京都市下京区七条御所ノ内本町98
== アクセス == * [[JR|西日本旅客鉄道]][[東海道本線]]（[[JR京都線]]） [[西大路駅]]より徒歩約4分
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * 若一神社由緒
== 関連項目 == * [[若一王子]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ 若一神社]
{{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}}

Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=draft}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
