{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

revision.comment: Bot: update category → History of Shimogyo-ku, Kyoto
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|若一神社|version=100351179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[JR West|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>

Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[Nishioji Station]].

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg|250px]] |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|若一神社|version=100351179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Taira no Kiyomori]]
[[Category:Completion tier 3]] }}
[[sn:Nyakuichi Shrine]] [[ja:若一神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q123115002]] Wikidata name: Nyakuichi Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|qid=Q1077022}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|若一神社|version=100351179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123115002]] Wikidata name: Nyakuichi Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|WD=Q1077022}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123115002]] Wikidata name: Nyakuichi Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|WD=Q1077022}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].

=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]

<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway]]'s [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123115002]] Wikidata name: Nyakuichi Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|WD=Q1077022}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month

== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]] during the [[:en:Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|Tenshō Era]] (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]] in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—[[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Matsuo Ōkami]] * Inari-sha: deity—[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]], enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123115002]] Wikidata name: Nyakuichi Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation=[[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an Era]], 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|WD=Q1077022}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of [[:en:Nin'an|Nin'an]] (1166), Kiyomori visited [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]], where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]] (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.

Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>

== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}, at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to {{ill|Kumano|en|Kumano|WD=Q240462|lt=Kumano}}, aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of {{ill|Nin'an|en|Nin'an|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an}} (1166), Kiyomori visited {{ill|Kumano|en|Kumano|WD=Q240462|lt=Kumano}}, where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin}} (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province}} during the {{ill|Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|en|Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island}} in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—{{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Matsuo Ōkami}} * Inari-sha: deity—{{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}}, enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway}}'s {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].

== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway}}'s {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:若一神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Nyakuichi Shrine (若一神社) |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]], Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Nyakuichi shrine.jpg |Latitude degrees=34|Latitude minutes=59|Latitude seconds=3.9 |Longitude degrees=135|Longitude minutes=43|Longitude seconds=58.5 |Map Country Code= |deity=[[Nyakuichi Ōji]] |Foundation={{ill|Nin'an|en|Nin'an|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an Era|1=Nin'an}}, 1st year (1166), November 10 |type= |Pilgrimage Sites= |Main Hall= |festival= festival (Reisai) on November 10 |Ceremonies= |Map= Japan Kyoto city |prefecture={{ill|Shimogyō-ku|WD=Q1077022|1=Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.984417|N|135.732917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Nyakuichi Shrine''' (若一神社) is located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto]], [[:en:Shimogyō-ku|Shimogyō Ward]]. It stands on the site of the Nishihachijō Palace, once the private residence of [[Taira no Kiyomori]].
== deity == * Main deity: [[Nyakuichi Ōji]] (若一王子)
== History == During the reign of [[:en:Kōnin Tennō|Emperor Kōnin]], a monk named Ikō Shōnin arrived from [[:en:Tang Dynasty|Tang China]] and resided in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, at [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]. Ikō traveled to {{ill|Kumano|en|Kumano|WD=Q240462|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano}}, aiming to aid people, and carried with him a sacred object representing the divided spirit of [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Jūnisho Gongen]], specifically [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]. In the 3rd year of the [[Hōki Era]] (772), he arrived in the area and placed the sacred object in an old hall within the local forest, marking the shrine's origin.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine">[http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/index.html Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]</ref>
Later, the sacred object is said to have been buried underground due to mysterious circumstances.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
During the late [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]], [[Taira no Kiyomori]] resided at his Rokuhara Mansion and built the Nishihachijō Palace in the scenic area. In August of the 1st year of {{ill|Nin'an|en|Nin'an|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} (1166), Kiyomori visited {{ill|Kumano|en|Kumano|WD=Q240462|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano}}, where he received a divine message instructing him to recover and enshrine the buried sacred object. Upon his return to Kyoto, he identified a light emanating from a mound on the palace grounds, excavated the area, and uncovered the sacred object. On November 10 of the same year, he established the shrine as a guardian shrine.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
In February of the 2nd year of Nin'an (1167), Kiyomori was appointed {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}} (Grand Minister of State). The shrine subsequently gained reverence as a deity of fortune and success.<ref name="Nyakuichi Shrine"/>
The shrine grounds feature a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] planted by Taira no Kiyomori himself. During the extension of the Kyoto City Streetcar in 1939, the tree was spared due to fears of Kiyomori's curse, causing the streetcar route (and later [[Nishioji Street]]) to curve around it.<ref name="KYOTO design">[https://kyoto-design.jp/spot/3539 KYOTO design Nyakuichi Shrine]</ref>
== Grounds == * Main Hall * Worship Hall * Shrine Office * Sacred Water associated with [[Taira no Kiyomori]] * Statue of Taira no Kiyomori * Kiyomori's Camphor Tree - Approximately 800 years old, planted in gratitude after his appointment as Daijō-daijin. Located across the street from the shrine, the tree influenced the layout of Nishioji Street.
=== Auxiliary Shrines === * Jūmyō-sha: Deities—Takasago Jō and Uba. Enshrined from {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasago Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}} during the {{ill|Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|en|Tenshō_(Momoyama_period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} (1573–1592). * Benzaiten-sha: deity—[[Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto]]. Enshrined from [[Tsukubusuma Shrine]] on {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}} in [[Shōtoku Era]], 5th year (1715). * Matsuo-sha: deity—{{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Matsuo Ōkami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} * Inari-sha: deity—{{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}, enshrined from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]]. * Soreisha: Deities—Spirits of shrine priests, leaders, and revered patrons. Established in [[:en:Shōwa Era|Shōwa Era]], 58th year (1983), on September 11. * Kusunoki-sha: deity—Kiyomori's camphor tree.
<gallery> NyakuichiJinjyaTairanoKiyomori.jpg|Statue of Taira no Kiyomori </gallery>
== Festivals == * festival (Reisai) - November 10 * Monthly Ritual (Tsukinami-sai) - 10th day of each month * Beginning-of-Month Ritual (Otsuitachi-mairi) - 1st day of each month * Seasonal Festival (Shunsai) - 21st day of each month
== location == * Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City, Shimogyō Ward, Gosho-no-uchi Honmachi 98
== Access == * Approximately 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway|1=West Japan Railway Company}}'s {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} (JR Kyoto Line) [[:en:Nishioji Station|Nishioji Station]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}}
=== Sources === * Shrine Origins, Nyakuichi Shrine == See Also == * [[Nyakuichi Ōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/05/010/ Nyakuichi Shrine Official Website]
